explanation: A report generator takes data from the database and formats it for printed or on-screen output. Updating files is done by DML or a data entry form, and data entry is the operator's job, so neither is a report generator's function.

Related fields grouped together form one record (for example the title, artist and year fields of one album). Going one level up, related records grouped together form a data file, which is why option A is a level too high.

DROP is the SQL DDL verb for removing a schema object, so DROP FUNCTION removes a stored function. The trap is DELETE, which is DML and removes rows from a table; it cannot remove the function itself. REMOVE and ERASE are not SQL keywords.

SELECT DISTINCT is the standard SQL clause that collapses duplicate rows so each distinct set of values is returned once. SELECT UNIQUE is only a legacy Oracle synonym and is not standard SQL, which is why 'All of the above' is wrong.

ALTER TRIGGER trigger_name ENABLE / DISABLE is the command that turns an individual trigger on or off. ALTER TABLE can enable or disable all triggers on a table at once in some dialects, but it is not the command that targets a trigger, and MODIFY TRIGGER does not exist.

Modifying a database means changing the stored data: insertion, deletion and updating. Sorting only changes the order in which rows are presented in a result set; the stored data is untouched, so it is not a modification.

DROP TABLE removes the relation itself - its rows, its structure and its definition in the schema. The classic trap is DELETE, which only removes rows and leaves the empty table in place; ALTER changes a table's structure but never removes it, and ERASE is not SQL at all.

Referential integrity constrains the CHILD table: every non-null foreign-key value must already exist as a primary-key value in the referenced parent table, so a foreign key can never point at a row that does not exist. The reversed option (every primary key must match a foreign key) is the trap - a parent row is perfectly legal with no children referencing it.

ALTER INDEX is the real SQL command for changing an existing index's properties, including its future storage allocation (e.g. ALTER INDEX ... STORAGE / REBUILD in Oracle). REDEFINE INDEX, MODIFY INDEX and DO INDEX are invented names and are not SQL commands.

Data integrity is defined as the correctness (accuracy, validity) and completeness of the data held in the database. The distractors are adjacent but distinct: security is about controlling who may access data, a constraint is one of the mechanisms used to enforce integrity, and data independence is about insulating applications from schema changes.

DML is the sub-language for working with the data itself: SELECT retrieves, INSERT adds, UPDATE changes and DELETE removes rows. Retrieval, insertion and deletion are all DML operations, so "All of the above" is correct; the trap is picking a single one and confusing DML with DDL (CREATE/ALTER/DROP), which acts on structure, not data.
